contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: A moving aggregate may use constant auxiliary state for one traversal, whereas reusable prefix or prefix-hash state spends O(n) memory; specialized windows can also require O(n) maps or deques, so representation-specific peak state must be counted.

items.prompt: A one-pass fixed-size sum window stores only boundaries and the current sum. What is its typical auxiliary-space advantage over a full prefix array?

options.text: O(1) moving state instead of O(n) cumulative storage.
Zero storage instead of O(1).
O(n) moving state instead of O(1) prefix state.
Both representations always use the same space.

feedback.reason: A rolling aggregate retains only information for the current interval.
feedback.details: A prefix array retains one cumulative value per boundary to support later random access.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_vs_constant: The window still stores aggregate and boundary state.

items.prompt: Why does a prefix array normally require O(n) auxiliary space?

options.text: It stores cumulative state for n + 1 interval boundaries.
It stores one value per future query.
It stores exactly k values for any query width.
It is a sorting buffer.

feedback.reason: The representation supports any later [l, r] by retaining cumulative information at every boundary.
feedback.details: That reuse is purchased with linear storage.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.query_count: Its size is tied to input boundaries, not q.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_width: A prefix array covers the whole input.

items.prompt: A prefix-hash exact-sum algorithm stores up to one entry per distinct cumulative value. What is its worst-case auxiliary space?

options.text: O(n).
O(n²).
O(log n).

feedback.reason: Historical lookup requires retaining cumulative values from earlier boundaries.
feedback.details: The worst case has a distinct prefix at every step.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.quadratic: Only n + 1 prefix boundaries are produced.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.logarithmic: No balancing invariant limits the number of keys.

items.prompt: Why is the claim "sliding windows always use O(1) space" false?

options.text: Some windows maintain frequency maps, deques, or other state whose size grows with the window or distinct values.
Two indices themselves use O(n) space.
Every window requires a prefix array.
Any O(n) time algorithm uses O(n) space.

feedback.reason: Sliding window describes boundary movement, not a universal payload size.
feedback.details: A sum window may be constant-space, while a distinct-element or monotonic-window variant can retain O(n) state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.boundaries_linear: A fixed number of indices is O(1).
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.time_space_same: Time and space are separate dimensions.

items.prompt: A shortest-subarray-at-least-k solution for arbitrary signed values uses prefix sums and a monotonic deque of prefix indices. What peak auxiliary-space bound is possible?

options.text: O(n).
O(n²).
No auxiliary space because the answer is one length.

feedback.reason: The prefix sequence and deque can each contain O(n) entries.
feedback.details: The result may be scalar while the algorithm's working state is linear.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: The deque may retain many candidate boundaries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.quadratic: There are only O(n) prefix boundaries.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.output_only: Working state is distinct from output.

items.prompt: The result must materialize all m requested interval sums. How should their storage be classified?

options.text: O(m) required output space, separate from auxiliary working space.
It is always auxiliary space and should be hidden.
It belongs to the original input array.
A scalar sum means all m outputs fit in O(1).

feedback.reason: Space analysis should distinguish required results from temporary state.
feedback.details: A streaming API could reduce retained output, but that is a different output contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.auxiliary_only: The caller explicitly requires the results.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.constant: Materializing m scalars uses linear output space in m.

items.prompt: A prefix array is built in a wider numeric type to avoid overflow. What changes asymptotically under a fixed-width machine model?

options.text: The auxiliary space remains O(n), although each entry may use a larger constant number of bytes.
Wider values make the array O(n²).
A safer type removes the need to store entries.
Overflow safety forces a sliding window.

feedback.reason: Asymptotic space counts how stored state grows with n.
feedback.details: Numeric-width safety can materially affect constants and correctness without changing O(n) under a fixed-word model.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.becomes_quadratic: A constant-size wider word changes only the constant factor.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_required: Both approaches need an adequate numeric type.

items.prompt: The data is immutable and many range queries must be answered, but O(n) auxiliary memory is forbidden. Which conclusion is safest?

options.text: Prefix preprocessing may be unavailable; use direct scans, a compressed/domain-specific representation, or change the resource contract.
A full prefix array has no memory cost because it is preprocessing.
One rolling sum answers all arbitrary ranges in O(1).
Time complexity always overrides a hard memory cap.

feedback.reason: A time optimization is not legal if its required state exceeds the memory budget.
feedback.details: The correct response is to expose the tradeoff rather than claim a free representation.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_free: Preprocessing state remains live auxiliary memory.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.window_random_o1: A moving state represents only one current interval.

items.prompt: Which structures contribute to peak auxiliary space in window or prefix solutions? Select all that apply.

options.text: A cumulative array retained for random queries.
A map of prior cumulative values.
A frequency map retained for the current window.
The pre-existing input as newly allocated auxiliary memory.

feedback.reason: Peak auxiliary space includes every live structure created to perform the computation.
feedback.details: Input and output should be reported separately.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.original_input: Input space is not created by the algorithm.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.window_map: Its size can grow with the active state.

items.prompt: Which window workloads can genuinely use O(1) auxiliary state? Select all that apply.

options.text: A fixed-size rolling sum.
A nonnegative threshold window maintaining only sum.
A fixed-size rolling count of a boolean property.
A window requiring frequencies for up to n distinct values.

feedback.reason: Constant space depends on the maintained payload, not merely on moving boundaries.
feedback.details: Distinctness constraints often require growing state.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.distinct_constraint: The frequency table can be O(n).

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.fixed_sum: Only sum and boundaries are needed.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.positive_threshold_sum: Two boundaries and one aggregate suffice.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.fixed_count: One count can be updated incrementally.

items.prompt: Which statements about prefix-state memory are correct? Select all that apply.

options.text: A full one-dimensional prefix array uses O(n) entries.
A prefix hash map can use O(n) entries in the worst case.
The retained space can support many later compatible queries.
Prefix storage is always required output.

feedback.reason: Prefix representations retain historical cumulative state for future arithmetic or lookup.
feedback.details: They are auxiliary unless the API explicitly returns them.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_output: It is usually auxiliary preprocessing state.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.array_linear: One cumulative value is stored per boundary.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.reusable: Reuse is the reason for storing it.

items.prompt: Which mistaken space claims confuse moving and prefix representations? Select all that apply.

options.text: Every sliding-window algorithm uses O(1) space.
Prefix sums trade time but not memory.
Required output and auxiliary space are the same category.
A simple fixed-size sum window can use O(1) auxiliary state.

feedback.reason: Space claims must name the actual retained representation and output contract.
feedback.details: A specific constant-state rolling sum remains valid.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rolling_constant_case: This is a valid specific claim.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.prefix_time_only: A full prefix representation uses linear state.

items.prompt: Arrange a peak-space audit.

elements.text: List every input-derived structure live at the same time.
Separate input, required output, and auxiliary working state.
Express maps, deques, arrays, and copies in n, k, or distinct count.
Report the maximum simultaneous auxiliary state, not the sum of allocations over time.

feedback.reason: Space complexity is about live storage, not cumulative allocation events.
feedback.details: Classification prevents required output from being mislabeled as algorithmic workspace.

items.prompt: Arrange the lifecycle of a reusable prefix array.

elements.text: Populate them from the immutable source.
Keep the array live across compatible queries.
Release it when the source version or workload ends.

feedback.reason: The O(n) state remains live because later queries depend on it.
feedback.details: Releasing after each query would destroy the preprocessing benefit.

items.prompt: Arrange the lifecycle of a constant-state rolling sum.

elements.text: Compute or seed the first current interval aggregate.
Replace outgoing contribution with incoming contribution.
Use the current aggregate before moving to the next interval.
Discard the aggregate after the single sequential traversal.

feedback.reason: Only one interval state is live at a time.
feedback.details: The method saves space by giving up arbitrary later interval access.

items.prompt: Compute all fixed-size window sums over n values with a rolling aggregate. Select time and auxiliary space excluding streamed output.
constraints: Each element enters and leaves the aggregate at most once.

feedback.reason: This is the canonical constant-state window case.
feedback.details: If the outgoing value is unavailable from the input stream, a k-sized buffer may be needed; the prompt assumes indexed input.

items.prompt: A mutable n-value numeric array is converted in place into cumulative prefix values and then serves q scalar range-sum queries. Select total time and additional auxiliary space.
constraints: Overwriting the original values is legal.
Each query uses constant-time prefix arithmetic.
The existing array storage is reused and output is scalar.

feedback.reason: Prefix state does not inherently require a second O(n) allocation when the input itself may become the cumulative representation.
feedback.details: The tradeoff is destructive mutation: original element values are no longer available in their initial form.

items.prompt: A variable window tracks at most d distinct values using a frequency map while both boundaries advance monotonically across n positions. Select expected time and auxiliary space.
constraints: The active window may contain d distinct keys.

feedback.reason: Monotone boundaries keep total updates linear, but the payload is not constant-sized.
feedback.details: This directly refutes the universal O(1)-space window heuristic.

items.prompt: A mixed-sign shortest-subarray algorithm builds n + 1 prefix values and maintains a monotonic deque of prefix indices. Select total time and peak auxiliary space.
constraints: Each prefix index enters and leaves the deque at most once.

feedback.reason: A stronger prefix structure can recover linear time after the simple window fails.
feedback.details: It does so with linear retained state.

items.prompt: Materialize m interval-sum answers using a prefix array. Select query-processing time, auxiliary space excluding the prefix array and output, and output space.
constraints: Each answer is appended to a result list.

feedback.reason: Existing preprocessing makes each query constant-time, but m answers still require m operations and m output slots.
feedback.details: Required output is not hidden as auxiliary space.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: A moving aggregate may use constant auxiliary state for one traversal, whereas reusable prefix or prefix-hash state spends O(n) memory; specialized windows can also require O(n) maps or deques, so representation-specific peak state must be counted.
